title: 2 — Bly
content: The rain starts twenty minutes out of Inverness.

I watch it streak across the windshield in silver threads, the wipers swinging left-right-left like a metronome marking time toward something I can't name. The Highlands unfold through the glass in gradients of gray and green, and I catalogue each shift the way I catalogue everything: city bleeding into suburb, suburb thinning to forest, forest deepening into something older. Something that was here before the roads were cut and will be here after they're reclaimed.
The driver hasn't spoken since I said the destination.

His hands tightened on the wheel when I said it. Bly Manor. Two syllables and his knuckles went white. He drives faster now, as if delivering me quickly means he can leave quickly, and I file that reaction where I file all reactions: under useful.

The road narrows. Stone walls appear on either side, thick with moss and lichen, and the trees press inward. Oak, I think. Ash. Things with roots that go down further than I want to think about. The car's headlights carve a tunnel through the rain-dusk even though it's only three in the afternoon. Up here, the light gives up early.

I should be nervous. I'm not. I should be prepared, and I am — I've spent years preparing for a place I'd never seen, filling in its outlines from grainy photographs and fragmentary descriptions and one very terse email from a solicitor who didn't want to answer my questions. I know the layout in theory. East wing. West wing. Central hall. The lake to the north.

But theory isn't stone. Theory isn't the way the air changes when you cross from one county to the next, the barometer of your body dropping by increments you can't articulate. I feel it in my ears. A pressure. Like descending.

The driver takes a left onto an unmarked road. Gravel sprays beneath the tyres. We pass through a gate — iron, rusted at the hinges, flanked by stone pillars wearing down to skulls — and the forest opens.
And there it is.
Bly.

The manor appears between the trees like a revelation. Like something the landscape was holding in its mouth and finally released. Turrets. Ivy. A black lake visible through the bare skeletons of willows on the north side. The stone is granite — gray shot through with silver, wet from the rain and gleaming like the hide of something alive. Windows tall and narrow. Too many of them dark. The photographs didn't capture this. The photographs were extraordinary, but they were static, dead things. This is — this presses. The architecture presses. The weight of it, the sheer mass of accumulated years, reaches out and puts its hands on my shoulders and pushes.

The car stops. The driver is already out, hauling my suitcase from the boot with the brisk efficiency of someone who wants to be gone. He sets it on the gravel and gets back behind the wheel without a word. Without looking at the house. The car reverses down the drive faster than is wise on wet gravel, and then I'm alone.
The rain has stopped. I didn't notice when.

I stand on the gravel and breathe. Wet stone. Pine. The green dark smell of growth and rot in equal measure. And under it — something else. Something I can't name. Something under the surface, like a lake that goes deeper than it looks. The air has a quality I want to call electric, except it's not buzzing. It's still. Completely, impossibly still, as if the manor exists inside a pocket where weather forgot to happen.
I pick up my suitcase. The gravel crunches under my boots. Each step seems too loud.
The door opens before I reach it.

Mrs. Grose. I know her name from the solicitor's email. Housekeeper. Twenty years at Bly. Nothing else — the solicitor was not a fountain of information. In person she's smaller than I expected, compact and neat, with iron-gray hair pulled back in a knot so tight it must give her headaches. Her hands are red from washing. She folds them in front of her apron as she takes my measure, and whatever conclusion she reaches, she keeps behind a smile that doesn't reach her eyes.
"Miss Bly. You'll want to settle in before the children come down."
Not a question. An instruction. I file that too.

She leads me through the central hall. The floor is black and white tile, cracked in places, polished to a shine in others. The walls are paneled in dark oak, hung with paintings I don't stop to examine — I'm mapping instead. Doorways. Hallways. The grand staircase curving upward. Two wings branching from the central hall, east and west, exactly as the layout promised. The air inside is colder than outside. Colder by degrees. My breath doesn't mist but it feels close.
We climb the stairs. Second floor. West wing. She stops at a door and opens it and stands aside.
"Your room. Supper's at seven. The children eat with you — Mr. Ashford's instructions."
"Mr. Ashford being—"
"Their uncle. He's abroad. You'll not meet him." She says it the way you say the sky is gray. Factual and final and a little sad.

The room is beautiful in the way Bly is beautiful: too much, too old, too heavy. A four-poster bed with curtains the color of dried blood. A writing desk by the window, overlooking the lake. A wardrobe that smells of cedar and something fainter beneath it. The wallpaper is a faded floral — roses, I think, or they were roses once, before time leeched the color out.
And the mirror.

It hangs above the vanity, tall and tarnished, and there's a crack running through it. Not a clean break — a fracture that branches like a river delta from a point near the upper right corner, splitting the glass into shards that still hold together by some stubborn refusal to fall. When I look into it, my face is divided. Two of me. One on either side of the break, the crack running through my left eye.
Mrs. Grose sees me notice it.
"Old house." She's moving already, pulling the curtains wider, adjusting a lamp. Her hands never stop. "Everything's a bit broken."
She leaves. I stand in the doorway and watch her go — straight-backed, efficient, not looking back — and then I close the door and I'm alone.

The first thing I do is check the room. Every corner. The wardrobe — empty, cedar, that fainter smell stronger now, something like lavender but cold, the way a scent goes cold when it's been sitting in a closed space for months. Under the bed. Behind the curtains. The window latches. The door lock. It works. Small mercies.
Then the drawers.

The top drawer of the vanity is empty except for a hairpin. Tortoiseshell. Old. The kind of thing that gets left behind when someone leaves quickly, or doesn't leave at all but stops needing hairpins. I hold it up to the cracked mirror. In the fractured glass, it looks like two hairpins.
I put it back. Close the drawer.
The lavender smell clings to my fingers.

Dinner is in the dining room. East wing, first floor. I find it by following the sound of cutlery being laid — Mrs. Grose, moving through the room with the same relentless industry, placing knives and forks at precise intervals on a table long enough to seat twenty. She's set three places at one end. Candles. Linen napkins. A bottle of wine already breathing.
"The children will be down directly," she says, and exits through a door I mentally tag as kitchen-adjacent.

I sit. The chair groans beneath me — old wood, old joints. The room is paneled like the hall, darker here, and the candlelight turns everything amber and shadow. Paintings on the walls. Faces. Generations of Ashfords, I assume, watching me with the mild disinterest of the dead.
The wine is estate-bottled. I pour myself a glass and taste it — dry, dark, with a mineral edge like licking a wet stone. Above me, footsteps. Light and quick. Two sets, slightly out of sync.
They descend the staircase like a painting come to life.

Miles first. He's wearing a velvet waistcoat — dark green, buttoned precise — over a white shirt with the collar open at the throat. Trousers pressed. Shoes polished. His hair is dark, cut neat, and his face is a thing of unsettling symmetry: wide-set eyes, sharp cheekbones, a mouth that sits in a neutral line as if smiling would be a decision he hasn't yet made. He's twelve. Maybe thirteen. He looks older. Not in body — in bearing. The way he holds himself, weight balanced, chin level, taking the measure of the room before his feet touch the floor.

Flora follows. A white dress with a blue sash. Hair in two braids. She's smaller than Miles by half a head, and her face is the opposite of his: round, soft, designed for tenderness. She should look like a child. She looks like a performance of a child. The dress is too clean. The braids are too even. The smile she gives me is too practiced, a gesture rehearsed until it achieved the correct shape.
They stand at the foot of the stairs, side by side, and watch me.
"Miss Bly," Miles says. His voice is clear. Careful. A voice that has been trained to be pleasant. "Welcome to Bly."
"Thank you." I keep my own voice even. I've worked with children before. Adolescents. Trauma cases. I know the signs. I know how to read what they're not saying. "It's good to be here."
"You studied psychology." Flora says it flatly, not a question.
"At Edinburgh. Yes."

Miles pulls out a chair for his sister. She sits. He takes the seat beside her. They face me across the table, and the candlelight makes their eyes gleam — an optical effect, I tell myself, just the reflection of flame on wet surfaces, nothing more.
"You must find us very interesting," Flora says.

The word interesting does things in her mouth. She shapes it slowly, tasting each syllable, and I realize with a cold prickle at the base of my skull that she's not asking. She's testing. She's running an experiment, and I'm the subject.
"I find my work interesting," I say. "I haven't started my work yet."

Miles has a pocket watch. He pulls it from his waistcoat and checks the time — a gesture so deliberate it has to be performed, a signifier of adulthood he's wearing like costume jewelry. He snaps it shut.
"Seven-oh-three," he says. "Punctual. Miss Jessel was always late."

The name drops into the room like a stone into still water. I don't react. I don't let myself react. I sip the wine instead — that wet stone taste again, cold minerals on my tongue — and set the glass down with care.
"I didn't know Miss Jessel."
"No," Miles says. "You wouldn't."
Flora picks up her fork. The tines catch the candlelight. She examines them as if checking for cleanliness, then sets the fork down again, food untouched.
On the sideboard behind them, I notice it now: a phone mounted on a small tripod, lens pointed at the table. A red light blinking. Recording.

The room changes shape around me. Not physically — nothing so dramatic. But the walls seem to lean inward, the ceiling to lower, the candlelight to narrow until everything outside the three of us falls away. They're filming dinner. They film themselves eating. This is normal to them. This is routine.
"Does the camera bother you?" Miles asks. He hasn't touched his food either. Neither of them have.
"Should it?"
"Miss Jessel asked us to turn it off." Flora's smile again. Sweet and curving and wrong. "We didn't."

I eat. I make myself eat — roasted root vegetables, something with rosemary, the taste grounding me in my body when my mind wants to drift toward the implications. Three places at a table for twenty. A phone recording an empty room. Two children who speak in statements and ask questions that aren't questions.
Flora wears a locket. Silver, oval, worn smooth at the edges from handling. Her thumb finds it now, rubbing the face of it the way you'd rub a worry stone.
"What's in the locket?" I ask.
Her hand drops. The smile doesn't falter but her eyes change — a shutter closing, fast as a blink.
"A picture," she says. "Of someone who went away."

Mrs. Grose clears the plates. Her hands don't stop moving — scraping, stacking, wiping. She doesn't look at the phone. She doesn't look at the children. She looks at me, once, and in her face I see something I recognize: the expression of a woman who has decided not to know things.
After dinner, the twins excuse themselves with formal precision. Miles offers a slight bow. Flora offers her hand, and when I take it, her fingers are cold and dry and grip mine a beat too long.
"It was lovely to meet you, Miss Bly."
"The pleasure was mine, Flora."

She releases my hand. They ascend the staircase in tandem, their footsteps syncing as they climb, and the sound of it — left-right-left-right, perfect unison — follows me into the west wing like a heartbeat.
In my room, I stand at the window.

The lake. Black glass. Not a ripple. The willows trail their branches in the water's edge like fingers trailing in ink, and between them a mist rises — white and thin, a veil drawn across the north shore. The grounds must slope down to the water. I can see the path, gravel giving way to grass giving way to reeds. A boathouse, half-hidden. And beyond the lake, the forest picking up where it left off, closing the manor in its jaws.
The mist thickens. I watch it shift and curl, and in the curling, a shape resolves.
A figure. Standing at the water's edge.

A woman. Dark hair hanging past her shoulders. Dark dress — long-sleeved, high-collared, the kind of dress that belongs in a different century, or in a wardrobe that hasn't been opened in years. She's facing the water. Her back is to me. She's not moving.
My breath fogs the glass. I blink.
The figure is gone.

The lake is empty. The mist is gone too — dissolved as suddenly as it formed, leaving only the black water and the willows and the darkness beyond. My reflection stares back from the window: a pale oval floating against the night, divided by the central mullion into two halves.
I turn from the window.
Flora stands in the doorway.

I didn't hear her approach. The floorboards in this hallway creak — I catalogued them earlier, the way I catalogue everything, mapping the safe routes and the loud ones. No one crosses this hallway silently. No one. But she's there, barefoot on the runner carpet, white nightgown, braids loosened for sleep, and she must have walked the entire length of the west wing without making a sound.
"You saw her, didn't you?"
My heart is hammering. I force my voice level. "Saw who?"

"Miss J." Flora says it with the casual ease of someone naming a friend. A neighbor. A presence so familiar she doesn't need a full name. "She waits by the lake. She's waiting for someone to find her."

The hallway behind her is dark. The doors along it are closed. I can see the landing at the far end, the staircase curving down into the black well of the central hall. Nothing moves. Only Flora, small and white and luminous in the doorframe like something the dark exhaled.
"Flora. It's late. You should be in bed."
She smiles.

Sweet. Angelic. Utterly, thoroughly wrong — a smile with no architecture behind it, no reason, no referent. A smile that exists because she has decided it should, and the decision is visible in the crispness of its edges.
"Goodnight, Miss Bly."

She pads away down the corridor. Bare feet on carpet. No sound. The darkness accepts her one step at a time — an ankle, a hip, a shoulder, the white of her nightgown, the dark of her hair — until she is gone and I am standing alone in the doorway of a dead woman's room, and the cracked mirror behind me is reflecting the window, and the window is reflecting the lake, and the lake is black and empty and waiting.
